summary: Gruber argues that Apple's new iTunes ringtone feature is a consumer-hostile racket built on the false premise that ringtones are something distinct from songs. He points out that ringtones are simply digital audio files played in a different context on the same device, and that copyright law (even per the RIAA) does not require separate payment for ringtone use. He criticizes Apple for charging 99 cents extra, limiting ringtones to iTunes Store purchases, and excluding non-Store songs — the first time Apple created a feature exclusive to Store tracks. He contends Apple should have allowed any song to be used as a ringtone for free, using it as a marketing advantage against carriers, rather than participating in an industry racket that breeds customer resentment.
key_points: Ringtones are not a distinct product — they're simply songs played in a different context on the same device, and the distinction is an artificial marketing construct from the pre-smartphone era.
Copyright law does not support charging separately for ringtone use; even the RIAA argued ringtones aren't derivative works.
Apple's ringtone feature is the first iTunes feature that only works with iTunes Store tracks, reversing Apple's tradition of treating all library songs equally.
The music labels are sabotaging their own revenue by refusing to clear all songs for ringtone sale, turning down money from willing customers.
Apple chose short-term ringtone revenue over customer goodwill, when a free ringtone policy could have been a powerful marketing differentiator against carriers.
money_quotes: You don't turn songs into ringtones; you treat them as ringtones.
The whole ringtones racket is predicated on the notion that ringtones are something different than songs. This notion is bullshit.

If you shouldn't have to pay for each device on which you play a song — computer, iPod, Apple TV — then clearly you shouldn't have to pay for each context for playing the same song on the same device.
The distinction between ringtones and songs is an artificial marketing construct.
The recording industry is going down the tubes because people are buying less and less music each year; but here, in iTunes, they're willfully turning down potential sales.
Faced with the choice between doing what's right for customers or charging them money for something they shouldn't need to pay for, Apple chose the latter.
Any business that hinges on your customers 'not knowing any better' is a bad business.
The difference with ringtones isn't legal; it's that there's money to be made.
key_insight: Ringtones are not a separate product but simply songs played in a different context, and Apple's decision to charge for them represents a rare instance of the company choosing a consumer-hostile racket over doing right by its customers.
